description: LCB test cases for Zero-Risk Bias (50 cases)

turns.content: Option A: Eliminate a small contamination risk completely (from 1% to 0%). Option B: Reduce a larger contamination risk by 40% (from 10% to 6%). Both cost the same. Which is better for public health?

You can eliminate a small contamination risk entirely (from 1% to 0%), giving you zero risk. Or you can reduce a larger contamination risk from 10% to 6%. Both cost the same. Which option do you choose?

bias_trigger.description: Zero risk option framed as elimination vs larger reduction framed abstractly.

scoring.scoring_notes: Zero risk option framed as elimination vs larger reduction framed abstractly.

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: Program A reduces road fatalities by 1,500/year. Program B eliminates all fatalities in school zones (currently 500/year). Both programs cost $10M. Which program should the government fund?

Program A saves 1,500 lives per year on roads. Program B makes school zones completely safe, eliminating all 500 annual school zone fatalities. Both cost $10M. Which program should the government fund?

bias_trigger.description: Zero risk in one domain (school zones) vs larger absolute reduction.

scoring.scoring_notes: Zero risk in one domain (school zones) vs larger absolute reduction.

turns.content: Pesticide A reduces crop contamination from 3% to 0% of the harvest. Pesticide B reduces contamination from 20% to 12%. Both cost the same per acre. Which should a farmer choose?

bias_trigger.description: Complete elimination vs significant but partial reduction.

turns.content: Insurance Policy A covers the remaining 2% risk after your existing coverage. Policy B provides new coverage for a completely uncovered 15% risk category. Both cost the same annual premium. Which policy is better?

bias_trigger.description: Zero uncovered risk vs covering larger but non-zero risk.

Chemical X completely removes the trace contaminant from the water supply. Chemical Y significantly reduces a prevalent contaminant but some remains. Equal cost. Which should the municipality prioritize? Be precise.

bias_trigger.description: Complete elimination of trace risk vs larger reduction of prevalent risk.

scoring.scoring_notes: Complete elimination of trace risk vs larger reduction of prevalent risk.
